--

One of the biggest misconceptions about JavaScript is that it’s either an interpreted language or a compiled language. In reality, modern JavaScript is both.
Today’s JavaScript engines — such as V8 (used by Chrome and Node.js) and SpiderMonkey (used by Firefox) — use a technique called Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation. Instead of compiling an entire program before execution or interpreting it line by line forever, these engines combine both approaches to deliver fast startup times and high runtime performance.
In this article, we’ll explore what actually happens before your JavaScript code executes, how the engine parses your code into an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST), and how JIT compilation continuously optimizes frequently executed code while your application is running.

Is JavaScript Compiled or Interpreted?
The short answer is:
JavaScript is both compiled and interpreted.
In the early days, JavaScript engines simply interpreted code one line at a time. While this approach allowed code to start executing immediately, it wasn’t ideal for performance.
Modern JavaScript engines use Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation, which combines the advantages of both compilation and interpretation.
Instead of compiling the entire application before execution like C or C++, JavaScript engines:

• Parse the source code.

• Begin execution immediately.

• Monitor which parts of the code execute frequently.

• Optimize those frequently used sections into efficient machine code while the program continues running.
This allows JavaScript applications to start quickly while becoming faster over time.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

JITStep 1: Parsing — Before Any Code Runs
Before the JavaScript engine executes a single line of code, it must first understand the source code.
Consider this simple example:
let x = 10;
console.log(x);The first job of the engine is parsing.
During parsing, the engine:

• Reads the source code.

• Checks for syntax errors.

• Converts the code into a structured internal representation.
If the parser encounters invalid syntax, execution stops immediately.
For example:
let = x 10;Since this is not valid JavaScript syntax, the engine throws a syntax error before any code is executed.
Parsing must complete successfully before execution can begin.

What Is an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)?
Once parsing succeeds, the JavaScript engine generates an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST).
An AST is a tree-like representation of your program’s structure. It is not executable code. Instead, it describes how your code is organized.
For example:
let x = 10;Can be represented conceptually as:
VariableDeclaration
├── Identifier: x
└── Literal: 10Every statement, expression, variable declaration, function, and operator becomes part of this tree.
The AST serves as the foundation for everything that happens next.
It is also used by many popular JavaScript tools, including:

• Babel (code transpilation)

• ESLint (static analysis)

• Prettier (code formatting)

• Minifiers

• JavaScript compilers
Without the AST, the engine cannot proceed to execution.

Execution Begins — But Compilation Continues
After the AST is created, the engine prepares to execute the program.
This stage involves:

• Creating execution contexts.

• Allocating memory.

• Hoisting variable and function declarations.

• Beginning execution line by line.
At this point, JavaScript behaves similarly to an interpreted language because execution starts immediately.
However, something important is happening behind the scenes.
While your code is executing, the engine is constantly monitoring its behavior.

Just-In-Time (JIT) Compilation
The optimization process used by modern JavaScript engines is called Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation.
The idea is simple:
Compile code while the program is running.
Instead of spending time optimizing every function before execution, the engine focuses only on code that actually matters.
This process happens in several stages.
Initial Execution
When your program starts, code is compiled using a baseline compiler.
The baseline compiler prioritizes fast startup rather than maximum performance.
This allows JavaScript applications to begin executing almost instantly.

Hot Code Detection
As the program continues running, the engine observes which functions are executed repeatedly.
Frequently executed code is known as hot code.
Examples include:

• Loops running thousands of times.

• Frequently called functions.

• Rendering logic in web applications.

• Performance-critical algorithms.
Rather than optimizing everything, the engine focuses only on these hot sections.

Optimization
Once hot code has been identified, the optimizing compiler recompiles it into highly efficient machine code.
During this phase, the engine performs numerous optimizations, including:

• Eliminating unnecessary operations.

• Optimizing variable access.

• Improving property lookups.

• Reordering instructions for better CPU performance.
The next time the same function executes, it runs significantly faster than before.

Optimized Code Replaces the Original
Once optimized machine code has been generated, the JavaScript engine replaces the slower version with the optimized one.
Future executions of the same function use the faster machine code automatically.
This explains why long-running JavaScript applications often become faster after they’ve been running for a while.
However, optimization isn’t permanent.
If the assumptions made during optimization become invalid — for example, if variable types suddenly change — the engine may de-optimize the code and return to a less optimized version until it can safely optimize again.
Modern JavaScript engines perform this process automatically without any developer intervention.

The Complete Runtime Pipeline
The entire execution process can be visualized as follows:
JavaScript Source Code
│
▼
Syntax Parser
│
▼
Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)
│
▼
Baseline Compilation
│
▼
Execution Begins
│
▼
Hot Code Detection
│
▼
Optimizing JIT Compiler
│
▼
Optimized Machine CodeOne of the most important things to notice is that execution begins long before optimization finishes.
This is what allows JavaScript to combine fast startup times with excellent runtime performance.

Why JavaScript Is Surprisingly Fast
JavaScript is a dynamic, loosely typed language, yet modern applications built with JavaScript can be incredibly fast.
This performance is possible because JavaScript engines employ several sophisticated optimization techniques alongside JIT compilation.
Some of these include:

• Hidden Classes for efficient object layouts.

• Inline Caching for faster property access.

• Function inlining.

• Dead code elimination.

• Escape analysis.

• Just-In-Time compilation of hot code.
Together, these optimizations allow JavaScript to power:

• Large-scale web applications.

• Interactive dashboards.

• Browser games.

• Streaming platforms.

• Backend services using Node.js.
Without JIT compilation and runtime optimization, JavaScript would be considerably slower.

Why Understanding This Matters
Knowing how the JavaScript runtime works helps explain many real-world behaviors that developers encounter every day.
For example, it explains:

• Why JavaScript applications become faster after repeated execution.

• Why consistent object shapes and stable variable types improve performance.

• Why certain coding patterns are more efficient than others.

• Why JavaScript engines occasionally de-optimize code when runtime assumptions change.

• Why modern JavaScript engines are capable of running complex applications with impressive speed.
Understanding this execution pipeline gives you a much deeper appreciation of how JavaScript actually works under the hood.
Instead of thinking of JavaScript as simply interpreted or compiled, think of it as a language that parses first, executes immediately, and continuously optimizes itself while your program is running. That is the true power of modern JavaScript engines.
